data.title: Developing|HSBC suffers system shutdown, stopping services in Hong Kong
data.description:
HSBC, Hong Kong’s biggest bank with a customer base of 6.2 million, saw a system breakdown on Friday afternoon.
The bank’s mobile banking app could not be accessed, instead showing a message stating the system was under maintenance and would be available at a later time.
A staff member at the bank’s Mong Kok branch said HSBC’s computer network across the city had failed, but did not know the reason for the stoppage.
An official with the bank also confirmed there had been a breakdown, but the system began resuming service around 3pm.
Official comments from HSBC or the Hong Kong Monetary Authority were not immediately available.
More to follow...

data.title: Nvidia wins approval for Taiwan headquarters to secure AI chip supplyJensen Huang’s expansion plan highlights Silicon Valley chip designer’s reliance on TSMC as AI demand accelerates.
data.description:
The Taiwan government has cleared Nvidia to establish a NT$3.3 billion (US$105 million) headquarters on the island that produces most of the world’s advanced semiconductors, which the Silicon Valley chip designer will need to feed the red-hot global artificial intelligence sector.
Nvidia would use the Taipei site for a “commercial office” building and the acquisition of land for a “comprehensive business park”, the island’s Ministry of Economic Affairs announced on Wednesday.
The approval came a day before Taiwan media reported that Nvidia founder and CEO Jensen Huang was due to arrive for events that include a potential meeting with C C Wei, chairman and CEO of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC), the world’s largest contract chipmaker.
Although neither company confirmed a meeting, analysts said Nvidia – reportedly TSMC’s biggest client after displacing Apple – had to ensure sufficient capacity to meet global demand for AI-enabled chips that power data centres and cloud services.
“Production capacity is booked well in advance,” said Stefan Angrick, associate director and senior economist at Moody’s Analytics. “Taiwan is the world’s dominant producer of advanced semiconductors, and it makes sense now that AI is bleeding into all kinds of devices.”

TSMC, founded in 1987, manufactures chips at or below the 5-nanometre process, which enables faster computing with lower power use. The firm produced around 70 per cent of the world’s chips last year, according to tech market consultant Counterpoint Research.
Demand is so strong that TSMC has allocated US$52 billion to US$56 billion for 2026 capital expenditure. On Tuesday, Taiwanese officials flashed a “red light” across a list of economic indicators – a signal that the economy was overheating.
TSMC told the SCMP on Thursday that as process technology became more complex, the “engagement” with customers would require a lead time of two to three years.
Global capital spending on AI infrastructure was expected to rise by more than 34 per cent this year, “sustaining strong demand” for semiconductors and related equipment, Swiss private bank Union Bancaire Privee said in an outlook on Tuesday.
Nvidia’s Huang outlined the Taiwan expansion during a speech at the Computex Taipei tech show in May, citing partnerships with Taiwanese tech firms as a key reason for the move. He said Nvidia had already secured a lease for a property and that the company’s engineering staff on the island had outgrown the existing office.
“As design complexity escalates, Nvidia needs a massive local R&D [research and development] team to collaborate with TSMC daily to solve yield challenges and accelerate time-to-market for next-generation silicon,” said David Wu, a research analyst at Counterpoint.
Taiwanese media reported on Thursday that the ministry’s approval followed Taipei’s termination of a land contract with an insurance company. The city also approved plans to merge two lots into one.
Huang had spent the past six days in mainland China for meetings, as Nvidia’s H200 – its second most powerful AI chip – was awaiting regulatory clearance.
TSMC would need to add a large amount of capacity over the next decade, Huang said, according to a news report by mainland-based Cailianshe on Thursday.
Nvidia’s Taiwan hub would allow the company to better manage a “complex” AI server supply chain involving “key partners” in mainland China and Southeast Asia, Wu said.

data.title: NWD shares jump after market views likely stake sale to Blackstone as positive
data.description:
Blackstone’s potential acquisition of a stake in New World Development (NWD) will help the embattled Hong Kong-listed developer improve its balance sheet and liquidity, benefiting creditors and shareholders alike, according to analysts.
The New York-based private equity giant’s investment in NWD could take the form of “shareholder loans or direct equity financing, as well as offloading noncore investment properties”, said Jeff Zhang, an equity analyst at Morningstar. “That said, uncertainties remain on the deal structure, as the continued rally in NWD’s share price may curb the size of Blackstone’s share purchase.”
NWD’s shares rose 4.4 per cent on Friday, taking the gains to nearly 57 per cent this month amid investor speculation about the stake sale. The Cheng family, one of the city’s wealthiest clans, owns 45.33 per cent of NWD.
NWD said late on Thursday that its parent, Chow Tai Fook Enterprises (CTFE), had been approached by potential investors after reports that CTFE could sell part of its stake to Blackstone.

Bloomberg reported earlier that Blackstone – the world’s biggest asset manager – was in discussion to become the single largest shareholder of NWD, citing people familiar with the matter.
Under the proposed arrangement, the US alternative asset manager would lead a restructuring of NWD, with the developer selling assets to improve liquidity. The size of Blackstone’s potential stake was not disclosed, the report said.
In a late-night filing to the Hong Kong stock exchange, NWD said it was aware of media speculation about potential new investors and had made inquiries with CTFE, which confirmed it had been approached by several parties.
“The market is excited about any possible corporate or shareholders’ action,” Citi said in a note on Friday. “That said, a change in control can also be done via new share issue, rights issue or direct share sale, which could pose downside risk if new equity is raised or in case of a change in company strategies.”
Citi said it maintained its sell recommendation on the developer’s stock.
The US bank also noted NWD’s “emerging value” as it reduced its debt and Hong Kong’s residential property market recovered.
Apart from privatisation, “any corporate action could pose share dilution risk, but it is credit positive, in our view”, Citi said.
No agreements had been reached and there was no certainty that any transaction would proceed, the company said.
NWD has become Hong Kong’s most indebted developer after years of debt-fuelled expansion collided with a prolonged property downturn.
It reported a HK$16.3 billion (US$2 billion) loss in June 2025, widening 38 per cent from a HK$11.8 billion loss a year earlier.
The developer has accelerated asset disposals while pursuing debt swaps and bank refinancing. Since the start of 2025, it has sold more than 2,200 residential units, including parking spaces, raising over HK$34.8 billion – its largest cash haul in nearly five years.

data.title: Hong Kong police probe theft of 58 million yen at currency exchange shop
data.description:
Hong Kong police are investigating an alleged robbery in which 58 million yen (US$376,600) was stolen outside a currency exchange shop in Sheung Wan.
The force said a passerby reported the case at 9.47am on Friday, with initial investigations suggesting two men made off with money worth HK$2.94 million near 272 Des Voeux Road Central.
The pair, one believed to be in his twenties and the other in his teens, got into a private vehicle after taking the cash and fled the scene, according to police.
No one was injured.
The case followed another similar robbery in the same district last month.
Four armed men robbed a currency exchange shop of 1 billion yen in December. The shop was located at 181 Queen’s Road Central near Grand Millennium Plaza, and a suspect was eventually arrested.

data.title: How Tesla’s AI shift is prompting Chinese rivals to expand tech offerings
data.description:
Tesla’s falling car deliveries and declining profitability on the mainland have not stopped its Chinese electric-car rivals from imitating the US firm’s transition into an artificial intelligence entity.
Xpeng, Li Auto and Nio, dubbed as Tesla challengers over the past decade, have taken similar steps to diversify into emerging technologies and position themselves for the future of transport.
These Chinese firms are now building next-generation vehicles, flying cars, robots and chips to create their own ecosystems and offer consumers alternatives to existing transport options, competing against their US rival on these new fronts.
“Tesla’s influence on its Chinese EV counterparts is not fading,” said Phate Zhang, the founder of Shanghai-based data provider CnEVPost. “Tesla’s pivot from car production to AI-related technologies has already inspired some Chinese rivals to beef up their technological heft.”


Tesla said on Wednesday that it was in the process of transforming itself into a physical AI firm from a hardware-centric business after reporting lacklustre earnings and deliveries last year.
CEO Elon Musk said Tesla would halt production of the Model S and Model X cars, converting the plant where they are made in Fremont, California, to build humanoid robots.
The US carmaker posted a 46 per cent slump in net profit and an 8.6 per cent drop in deliveries last year. The company delivered 1.64 million cars worldwide last year, but was dethroned by Chinese electric-vehicle (EV) king BYD as the world’s largest pure electric carmaker.
BYD sold 2.26 million battery-powered EVs in 2025, an increase of 28 per cent from a year earlier. The Chinese EV builder also assembles plug-in hybrids.
“Tesla’s diminished role as an EV maker does not mean it has lost its status as an industry bellwether,” said Ding Haifeng, a consultant at Shanghai-based financial ­advisory Integrity. “Its autonomous driving and robotics capabilities are the two key areas Chinese technology firms are making tremendous efforts to catch up.”
Tesla was a front runner in China’s EV market in 2020 when it started producing the Model 3 in Shanghai. Its market share on the mainland topped 16 per cent that year. But its share dropped to 6.9 per cent in 2024 amid mounting com­petition from home-grown firms like Li Auto and Xpeng, which developed dozens of ­premium models with intelligent features similar to those in Tesla’s cars.
In 2025, Tesla’s deliveries in China fell 4.8 per cent from a year earlier to 625,698 units. The sales represented 4.8 per cent of the total EV sales nationwide, according to data from the China Passenger Car Association (CPCA).
It was the fifth-largest EV assembler on the mainland last year, behind BYD, Geely, Changan Automobile and SAIC-GM-Wuling.
New EV sales on the mainland accounted for about 70 per cent of the global total, CPCA data showed.
The mainland’s assemblers and supply-chain vendors are also at the vanguard of EV technology and production, buoyed by government support and consumers’ willingness to embrace innovation, according to analysts.
But Chinese companies have yet to prove that their intelligent cars fitted with self-driving systems are superior to Tesla’s Full Self-Driving system.
Musk has touted Tesla’s edge in AI and autonomous driving as a decisive factor that justifies the company’s lofty stock market valuation.
“Today any carmaker with an enterprising spirit must have ambitions to build itself into an AI firm,” said William Li, co-founder and CEO of Nio, in a media briefing earlier this month.
Last month, He Xiaopeng, CEO of Xpeng, said AI would soon drive significant societal changes. Xpeng, through its subsidiaries, is designing flying cars and robots, with plans to start mass production this year.
In November, He said Xpeng envisioned sales of 1 million robots by 2030, pledging to bring down production costs so that robots could become affordable for use in Chinese homes.
He made the remarks just days after Musk endorsed Iron, Xpeng’s next-generation robot, on social media while predicting Chinese companies would ­dominate the global robotics market alongside Tesla.

data.title: Hong Kong’s CK Hutchison risks losing Panama port rights after court ruling
data.description:
Hong Kong-based CK Hutchison Holdings, controlled by billionaire Li Ka-shing, risks losing its rights to operate major ports at both ends of the Panama Canal after the country’s Supreme Court ruled its subsidiary’s contract unconstitutional.
The decision was announced in a statement posted on the official social media account of the Panama Judiciary on Thursday local time.
In the statement, the Palace of Justice Gil Ponce said the concession contract for the development, construction, operation, administration and management of the Balboa and Cristobal ports between the state and the Panama Ports Company (PPC), a CK Hutchison subsidiary, was unconstitutional.
The statement added that the decision was reached “following extensive deliberation and discussion”.
CK Hutchison holds a 90 per cent stake in PPC, which had a 25-year concession to operate the Balboa and Cristobal ports that was renewed in 2021.
The South China Morning Post has reached out to CK Hutchison for comment.
The court’s ruling followed an audit conducted by Panama’s comptroller in January last year, which accused the company of depriving Panama of more than US$1.3 billion (HK$101 billion) through a decades-old ports concession that was “negotiated against the Republic”.
The audit was launched on January 20, the same day United States President Donald Trump was inaugurated.

According to the audit findings, the contract signed in 1997 with PPC ran counter to national interests and accused former officials of favouring private interests over those of the state.
The investigation claimed that PPC generated US$3.78 billion from its operations at the Balboa and Cristobal terminals between 1997 and 2023, while Panama received just US$236 million.
PPC called on the Panamanian authorities to respect the rule of law in August last year after the government took legal action in an attempt to nullify its port contracts.
Meanwhile, CK Hutchison has been seeking to sell its stake in the Panamanian company as part of a massive US$23 billion global deal.
In March last year, the company reached an in-principle agreement to sell an 80 per cent stake in its port assets to a Western consortium led by BlackRock and Terminal Investment Limited (TiL Group).
TiL Group is the terminal arm of the world’s largest container shipper, Mediterranean Shipping Company (MSC).




The original deal would have encompassed 43 terminals in 23 countries and represented a major shift in the ownership of global maritime infrastructure.
The proposed sale was announced only a month after Trump embarked on a trade war with mainland China at the beginning of his second term.
However, Beijing denounced the deal just 10 days after its announcement and called the sale to Western investors a “betrayal” of national interests – particularly those tied to the mainland’s Belt and Road Initiative, a long-term strategic effort to link various developing economies into a China-centred trade network, driven by Chinese-backed infrastructure projects abroad.
By late March 2025, the mainland’s State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR) had launched a comprehensive antitrust review, effectively halting the sale.
The sale remained unresolved at the end of 2025, nearly 10 months after it was first announced.

data.title: More seat belt confusion: does the new law cover all public buses in Hong Kong?
data.description:
The controversy over mandatory seat belts in Hong Kong has intensified after a former lawmaker said a government statement failed to specify that the rule only applied to new buses registered from January 25 this year, and called on authorities to clarify their approach to enforcement as passengers worried about unwittingly breaking the law.
Doreen Kong Yuk-foon, a member of the subcommittee that examined the proposed legislative amendment last year, told the South China Morning Post on Friday that a government press release on January 8 had caused confusion by stating that passengers had to wear seat belts regardless of whether the vehicles were newly registered, contradicting what was written in the law.
She was referring to the new regulation that took effect on Sunday, which requires passengers on public buses to put on seat belts.
Kong said lawmakers who scrutinised the proposal last September had been told that the new rule would only apply to new vehicles registered on or after January 25 this year, and that the government’s January 8 press release had “wrongly interpreted” the law.
“The press release was wrong in saying that ‘drivers and passengers occupying the seats of these vehicles (whether newly registered or not), where seat belts are fitted, are required to wear them.’ It obviously does not match the clauses [in the law],” Kong said.
“People will have to ask those who wrote this press release why they did this. I do not understand.”
According to section 8AB under the Road Traffic (Safety Equipment) Regulations, buses newly registered on or after January 25, 2026, must install seat belts for passengers.
Section 8D specifies that “a person must not ride as a passenger in a passenger’s seat of a bus to which regulation 8AB applies on any road, unless the person is securely fastened to the seat by means of a seat belt, if any, provided for the seat.”
It remains uncertain how many public buses were newly registered since Sunday.
Kong said that the law had been widely misread and the government should clarify its terms as soon as possible.
“The government’s original aim is to target the buses first registered on or after January 25, 2026. The rule’s coverage was intended to be very limited,” she said.
Some residents have also questioned online how anyone could tell if a bus was newly registered.
She said the focus of the legislative debate at the time was not on seat belts, but on regulating mobile devices placed in front of drivers.

Her remarks were made hours after she clarified in a social media post that passengers would not be criminally liable if they did not wear seat belts on buses that were registered before January 25.
“I hope the government will clarify the provisions to avoid the public mistakenly believing that the penalties for not wearing seat belts apply to all buses under the newly revised provisions,” she wrote.
Kong said on Friday that the public had criticised lawmakers for not doing a good job of vetting the legislation and that a resident had even asked her to clarify the new rule.
“I do not want to embarrass the government; I just want to tell the truth to the public,” she said.
According to the paper submitted to the Legislative Council last September, authorities would require vehicles first registered on or after January 25, 2026 to be equipped with seat belts, including all passenger seats in public and private buses.
“Passengers occupying the above-mentioned seats, where seat belts are fitted, will be required to wear them starting from 25 January 2026,” the paper stated.
The subcommittee on the subsidiary legislation held just two meetings.
At the time, Principal Assistant Secretary for Transport and Logistics Cheng Sze-ling said the seat belt requirement would be extended to apply to the rear seats of private minibuses and buses registered on or after January 25, 2026.
No lawmakers at the meetings asked the government to confirm or clarify whether passengers on existing buses would also be required to put on seat belts.
Anyone convicted of breaching the law faces a maximum fine of HK$5,000 (US$640) and up to three months’ imprisonment.
SCMP has reached out to transport authorities and bus companies for comment.
Confusion over the new rule prompted some lawmakers to urge the government to review the legislation after six months, with the public struggling in the first week of implementation with poorly fitted restraints, hygiene issues and concerns that early unbuckling constituted a breach of the law.
The backlash prompted city leader John Lee Ka-chiu to call for patience on Tuesday, saying the initial stage of implementation would focus on public education and “fostering habits through persuasion”.
Public controversy erupted on Thursday after a 40-year-old man was unable to unfasten his seat belt while preparing to get off a bus, leaving him trapped for 45 minutes before he was freed by firefighters.

data.title: AstraZeneca unveils US$15 billion China investment as Starmer visits Beijing
data.description:
British-Swedish pharmaceutical giant AstraZeneca said on Thursday it plans to invest US$15 billion in China through 2030, signalling renewed momentum in Sino-UK commercial ties.
The investment – the biggest deal announced so far during UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s trip to Beijing aimed at strengthening trade ties – will support expanded medicines manufacturing as well as research and development (R&D), the company said in a statement.
“Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements,” Starmer said in the statement, adding that AstraZeneca’s expansion in China would support thousands of UK jobs and reinforce the country’s life sciences sector.
Starmer’s four-day trip marks the first visit to China by a British prime minister in eight years and comes as London seeks closer economic cooperation with Beijing amid strained relations with Washington.
During talks on Thursday, Chinese President Xi Jinping called for a “new chapter” in Sino-UK relations and urged deeper dialogue and cooperation in what he described as a complex and volatile global environment, according to state news agency Xinhua.
Starmer said it was “crucial” for both countries to pursue a long-term and stable comprehensive strategic partnership, Xinhua reported.
“The multibillion pound investment announced today from AstraZeneca, alongside partnerships from some of our country’s leading universities, furthers research and development in the UK and helps power our world class life sciences sector,” said Starmer in the AstraZeneca statement.
AstraZeneca CEO Pascal Soriot described the investment as “an exciting next chapter” for the company in China, calling the country a growing force in scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.

AstraZeneca will expand its existing manufacturing plants in the eastern Chinese cities of Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao, as well as in Beijing, as part of its commitment.
The company said the money would go into scaling cell therapy alongside broader drug discovery, clinical development and high-end manufacturing.
CSPC Pharmaceutical Group, a Hong Kong-listed drug maker, is among AstraZeneca’s Chinese partners benefiting from the investment.
The two companies signed a collaboration and licence agreement to co-develop innovative medicines using CSPC’s AI-driven peptide discovery platforms, according to an exchange filing on Friday.
Under the deal, AstraZeneca would have exclusive global rights outside mainland China, Hong Kong, Macau and Taiwan to CSPC’s injectable weight-management portfolio. This includes SYH2082, which is moving into phase one trials and three preclinical obesity programmes.
CSPC will receive an upfront payment of US$1.2 billion and could earn up to US$3.5 billion in R&D milestones and another US$13.8 billion in sales milestones, in addition to double-digit royalties on annual net sales of the licensed products.
AstraZeneca’s announcement comes as China builds its reputation as a pharmaceutical innovation hub.
The pharmaceutical giant has also been under investigation in China since last year, including over alleged irregularities involving the import of oncology drugs.
The Shenzhen customs authority identified suspected unpaid import taxes related to Imfinzi and Imjudo, with potential fines of up to US$4.5 million, according to Chinese media reports.
Separately, authorities also flagged suspected unpaid taxes for the breast cancer drug Enhertu, which could result in fines of up to US$8 million, the reports said.
In December, US-based SAI MedPartners and its unit Idea Pharma launched the China Pharmaceutical Innovation and Invention Index, a stand-alone benchmark measuring the R&D capabilities of Chinese drug makers.
The firms had previously published only a global pharmaceutical innovation index for 14 years.
“The huge investment pledge appears to align with Starmer’s China visit, suggesting deeper collaboration between the two countries amid current geopolitical tensions,” said Jialin Zhang, head of China healthcare research at Nomura.
China is AstraZeneca’s second-largest market by sales and an increasingly important R&D base, Zhang said, adding that this dual role helped explain the scale of the new commitment.
“That explains why we see it investing so heavily right now,” he said.
Other healthcare analysts said the scale of the commitment also reflects China’s growing weight in the global pharmaceutical market.
“I’d say it is one of the largest I have seen in China,” said Tony Ren, head of Asia healthcare research, Macquarie Capital. “China is the second largest national drug market in the world. Most large pharma companies want to have some presence.”
Japanese pharmaceutical company Daiichi Sankyo also recently announced investments in Germany, Japan, the US and China, “underscoring the importance of all these large drug markets and in particular China,” said Ren.
“I do think other multinational companies are likely to do the same.”
The investments will significantly enhance AstraZeneca’s cell therapy and radioconjugates capabilities, which are driving its broad and varied pipeline to help patients with cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others, the firm said in the statement.
The investments, the company said, will span the value chain, from drug discovery and clinical development to manufacturing.
Additional reporting by Julie Zhang

data.title: Discovery of badly decomposed whale carcass on Sai Kung beach triggers probe
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities are investigating the discovery of a severely decomposed whale carcass discovered on a beach in Sai Kung.
Authorities were alerted to the decomposed carcass, measuring 11 metres (36.1 feet) in length, on Thursday after villagers found it on the beach at Ham Tin Wan, in Sai Kung East Country Park.
The Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department (AFCD) deployed officers to the scene, with preliminary findings suggesting that the animal was a baleen whale.
Based on the degree of decomposition, the carcass was believed to have been dead for some time before drifting from the open seas into the waters off Ham Tin Wan, it added.
The department said a necropsy could not be conducted due to its advanced decomposition, adding that its staff were working to dispose of the carcass and clean up the area by Friday.
It also reminded the public to stay away from the area to avoid obstructing the process.
Baleen whales typically forage in the cold, high-latitude waters around the Arctic and Antarctic during the summer, and migrate to warmer, low-latitude or tropical waters for breeding and nurturing their young in winter.
The Food and Environmental Hygiene Department said it would move the carcass to a designated location, where its appointed contractor would transport the remains to a landfill for disposal. A vessel was seen approaching the beach on Thursday night.
An Ocean Park spokesman confirmed that its staff and the AFCD had taken samples from the carcass for laboratory testing.
Viena Mak Hei-man, vice-chairwoman of the Hong Kong Dolphin Conservation Society, said the whale was already severely decomposed and was likely to have died about a week ago before drifting from the South China Sea to local shores.
Ham Tin Wan is a popular camping and hiking destination. The sight of the carcass also drew the attention of mainland Chinese visitors, with some stopping to take photos.

data.title: Hong Kong travellers continue to favour Japan despite geopolitical tensions
data.description:
Hong Kong travellers continue to favour Japan as a holiday destination despite ongoing geopolitical tensions between Beijing and Tokyo, thanks to unaffected flight routes and a weaker yen, local industry operators have said.
Travel agencies also identified mainland China as the top destination for Hong Kong travel groups, as they gathered at an exhibition on Thursday to promote packages for the coming spring season.
Beijing recently issued a warning to its citizens, cautioning them against travelling to Japan during the coming Lunar New Year holiday, as the row over Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s November comments about Taiwan rumbled on.
State broadcaster CCTV reported that February flights along 49 routes between China and Japan had been cancelled.
However, Moon Yau Moon-yee, assistant general manager at Sunflower Travel Services and vice-chairman of the Tourism Industry Employees’ General Union, said Hong Kong tour groups bound for Japan were largely unaffected by the tensions.
Flights from the city to Japan have not been affected, Yau said, adding that the weaker yen has further encouraged Hongkongers to spend there.
“Hong Kong residents’ enthusiasm for travelling to Japan has never faded,” he said. “Meanwhile, when flights from mainland China were cancelled, some travellers across the border would choose to fly from Hong Kong as flights here are not affected.”
Yau was speaking while attending the Hong Kong Holiday and Travel Expo, which runs from Thursday to Sunday.
While noting that around 70 per cent of Hongkongers travelled to Japan on their own, Yau said the mainland has become the top destination for his travel agency, with Xinjiang drawing the most interest, followed by Yunnan.
Another exhibitor, Wing On Travel, also said it did not see a decline in customer appetite for Japan-bound trips.
“This year we did receive more inquiries for long-haul mainland and South Korea trips, but there is no significant drop in [inquiries for] Japan,” a spokeswoman for the agency told the South China Morning Post.
At the expo, Wing On Travel was promoting packages for departures during the Lunar New Year holiday, as well as cherry blossom tours to Wuhan in central China, South Korea and Japan, the spokeswoman added.

Optimism was also shared by Okini Home, a Hong Kong-based holiday home rental platform under FM Investment Group.
The platform manages bookings for around 800 flats in Osaka and had gained around 500 new users from the expo as of Thursday afternoon.
“Impacts from politics are temporary,” said Amous Lee, CEO and a founding partner of the group, projecting that demand from Hong Kong tourists would remain consistently strong.
According to the Japan National Tourism Organisation, more than 2.5 million Hong Kong residents visited the country in 2025, a 6.2 per cent drop from the previous year.
The number of Hong Kong tourists to Japan plunged by nearly 37 per cent in July 2025 after rumours spread that a major earthquake was imminent.
At the expo’s opening ceremony, Secretary for Culture, Sports and Tourism Rosanna Law Shuk-pui projected an uptake in both inbound and outbound travel for the coming year.
“With the opening of the third runway of the Hong Kong airport, our flights have recovered to 81 per cent of the 2018-19 level. Looking into this year, this number would further rebound to 86 per cent,” she said.
“This will not only attract more travellers to Hong Kong, but will also bring more choices to our travel-loving residents.”

data.title: China’s cloud giants race to support Moltbot, the ‘AI that actually does things’
data.description:
China’s artificial intelligence hyperscalers have quickly added cloud support for Moltbot, formerly known as Clawdbot, an open-source AI agent that has exploded in popularity globally.
Billed as the “AI that actually does things”, Moltbot’s ability to autonomously execute tasks without the user directing each action has caused waves in the global developer community, briefly lifting shares of US web infrastructure giant Cloudflare in pre-market trading on Tuesday before they later retreated.
This week, three of China’s biggest cloud service providers – Alibaba Cloud, Tencent Cloud and ByteDance’s unit Volcano Engine – began offering access to Moltbot with support from their respective suite of models. Alibaba Cloud is the AI and cloud services unit of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the SCMP.
While the AI agent was initially only compatible with foreign messaging apps such as Telegram and WhatsApp, Chinese tech giants have enabled interaction through their respective services, including Alibaba’s workplace collaboration platform DingTalk and Tencent Holdings’ WeCom, the work version of super app WeChat.
Smaller cloud providers have also added Moltbot, including the cloud unit of JD.com and Shanghai-based UCloud.
Created by Austrian developer Peter Steinberger, Moltbot is able to send messages, delete emails and manage files on a user’s computer autonomously. The AI agent, which users can interact with through messaging apps, has drawn attention not only for its powerful task-execution abilities but also because it was developed using another AI coding tool, without Steinberger writing the code.

However, privacy concerns have accompanied its rise. Users are required to hand over large amounts of private data and control over personal applications, prompting some to turn to separate devices – such as Apple’s Mac Mini – to protect their main devices.
As an open-source tool, Moltbot can also be integrated directly into cloud service providers’ offerings, allowing them to host data themselves.
Zhuang Shanning, a PhD student at Tsinghua University, said he used Moltbot to organise his calendar in Feishu, ByteDance’s enterprise collaboration platform, known overseas as Lark.
“While tinkering with it, I suddenly realised something: the way we interact with AI is changing,” he said. “Before, it was ‘I go out and find the tools’ myself. Now it’s ‘I say one sentence, and it gets it done’.
“Further down the line, it may be ‘I don’t need to say anything’.”
However, the tool’s heavy token usage made it expensive to run, according to Poe Zhao, a Beijing-based tech analyst and founder of Hello China Tech. In response, some Chinese cloud providers like Volcano Engine have introduced fixed-subscription options.
The global AI industry is moving towards developing AI agents capable of autonomously executing tasks on behalf of users. However, their strong autonomy as well as privacy concerns have raised questions about their regulatory future, including in China.
In December, ByteDance’s new AI-powered agentic phone faced pushback from users and domestic rivals, with several of China’s most widely used apps restricting its access.
In its announcement on Wednesday, ByteDance warned users about Moltbot’s security and privacy risks and urged them to regularly check their permission settings. “As the tool can access all device data, digital accounts and connect to the internet via multiple ports … it is recommended to deploy Moltbot on a dedicated device rather than one containing sensitive information,” the TikTok owner said.

data.title: Hong Kong firm, Elior Group sign deal to set up aircraft dismantling centre
data.description:
A French aeronautics services company and a Hong Kong aviation engineering company signed a joint venture deal on Thursday to establish an aircraft dismantling and parts trading facility in the city, with operations targeted to begin within the year.
But the project’s launch will hinge on the availability of a separate plot of land with barge and truck access. This site is required for recycling fuselages once the parts that can be traded have been removed, to avoid a bottleneck in the entire workflow.
Norbert Marx, CEO of China Aircraft Services Limited (CASL), which specialises in aircraft maintenance in Hong Kong, highlighted at the memorandum of understanding signing ceremony that the market for end-of-life aircraft dismantling had significant growth potential.
“We believe the time is now ripe to expand into the highly promising fields of aircraft disassembly, dismantling, harvesting of high-value components, certification and parts trading,” Marx said.
“For each retired aircraft, from turbine engines to fuselage metals, around 90 per cent of materials and components can be recycled and reused for other aircraft.”
Marx added that he hoped the joint venture could establish an ecosystem.
Chairman and CEO Daniel Derichebourg of the French company Elior Group said the firm was one of the few industry operators with a “Continuing Airworthiness Management Organisation” certification, which could properly check disassembled aircraft components and verify their airworthiness for subsequent use in other aircraft.
“This accreditation enables this future joint venture to engage in regional or global trading of reusable aircraft parts – a market which we anticipate will see a substantial worldwide demand,” he said.
“While the [Asia-Pacific] region is the world’s fastest-growing aviation market, it lacks a fully integrated aircraft dismantling solution.
“The new CASL-Elior joint venture directly addresses this gap, enabling airlines across Asia to dismantle retiring fleets in Hong Kong.”

Marx said that space was already available at CASL’s hangar at Hong Kong International Airport, where it would take in a serviceable aircraft from which parts needed would be harvested.
Afterwards, it would be brought to the apron to be dismantled for the recycling of materials, he added.
“The aircraft will be cut into small pieces so that we can recycle the material, and these pieces will be brought to a different facility; they will not stay at the airport,” Marx said, adding that the aircraft would only be in the hangar for a few days before being sent away.
Asked if the facility for recycling would be located at To Kau Wan – a site on northwest Lantau Island – Elior Asia CEO Janet Cheung said that the company was still in talks with the city government but had so far received positive feedback.
“What we need [is] a place where there’s a pier because moving an aircraft to the recycling plant could be by barge or by truck,” she said, agreeing that not having the plot of land would mean a bottleneck in the aircraft dismantling operations.
The South China Morning Post previously reported that To Kau Wan was likely to be chosen for the facility, citing sources, as the site was close to the airport and offered convenient access to existing infrastructure, including roads and a pier that could accommodate barges.
Cheung said the government could help by regulating the industry through legislation to require dismantlement works to be handled by designated companies.
The move would also help ensure the provenance of recovered parts, ensuring other jurisdictions were happy to buy them, she said.
“All parts to be reused have to have a serial number. We would need to know which aircraft they came from, whether they have been serviced, and how much lifetime was left,” she said, stressing that it would be an issue if a dismantled part with unknown information resulted in any accidents.
CASL’s two largest shareholders are Hutchison CCF Investments and the China National Aviation Holding Corporation.
Elior Group, a subsidiary of the Derichebourg Group, counts Airbus, Boeing and Comac among its clients and runs a wide range of businesses across 11 countries, covering industries such as aviation, construction and food contracting services.
The global commercial aircraft disassembly, dismantling and recycling market is projected to grow from US$8.1 billion in 2023 to US$14.7 billion by 2033, with about 400 to 450 planes to be dismantled and recycled each year.

data.title: Hong Kong supervisor held over cruelty after pond clean-up kills 110 fish
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested a supervisor from a cleaning company for suspected animal cruelty after more than 110 fish died while a pond was being cleaned at a public housing estate in Tseung Kwan O.
The woman, a 51-year-old supervisor from Fai Kee Environmental Cleaning, was taken into custody on Wednesday.
She was later released on bail and was required to report back to police next month.
Six workers from the contractor removed about 200 fish and several turtles from the pond near King Min House at King Lam Estate on Monday.
The animals were placed into three containers, but investigators alleged the team failed to provide adequate aeration, relying on just one air pump.
The Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals later confirmed that more than 110 fish had died. Around 80 fish and four turtles survived.
Police said they received a report from a passer-by on Monday, prompting the investigation.
Tseung Kwan O district’s crime squad is handling the case.
Under the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance, anyone found guilty of causing unnecessary suffering to animals faces a maximum penalty of three years in prison and a fine of HK$200,000 (US$25,600).

data.title: China’s cloud giants race to support Moltbot, the ‘AI that actually does things’
data.description:
China’s artificial intelligence hyperscalers have quickly added cloud support for Moltbot, formerly known as Clawdbot, an open-source AI agent that has exploded in popularity globally.
Billed as the “AI that actually does things”, Moltbot’s ability to autonomously execute tasks without the user directing each action has caused waves in the global developer community, briefly lifting shares of US web infrastructure giant Cloudflare in pre-market trading on Tuesday before they later retreated.
This week, three of China’s biggest cloud service providers – Alibaba Cloud, Tencent Cloud and ByteDance’s unit Volcano Engine – began offering access to Moltbot with support from their respective suite of models. Alibaba Cloud is the AI and cloud services unit of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the SCMP.
While the AI agent was initially only compatible with foreign messaging apps such as Telegram and WhatsApp, Chinese tech giants have enabled interaction through their respective services, including Alibaba’s workplace collaboration platform DingTalk and Tencent Holdings’ WeCom, the work version of super app WeChat.
Smaller cloud providers have also added Moltbot, including the cloud unit of JD.com and Shanghai-based UCloud.
Created by Austrian developer Peter Steinberger, Moltbot is able to send messages, delete emails and manage files on a user’s computer autonomously. The AI agent, which users can interact with through messaging apps, has drawn attention not only for its powerful task-execution abilities but also because it was developed using another AI coding tool, without Steinberger writing the code.

However, privacy concerns have accompanied its rise. Users are required to hand over large amounts of private data and control over personal applications, prompting some to turn to separate devices – such as Apple’s Mac Mini – to protect their main devices.
As an open-source tool, Moltbot can also be integrated directly into cloud service providers’ offerings, allowing them to host data themselves.
Zhuang Shanning, a PhD student at Tsinghua University, said he used Moltbot to organise his calendar in Feishu, ByteDance’s enterprise collaboration platform, known overseas as Lark.
“While tinkering with it, I suddenly realised something: the way we interact with AI is changing,” he said. “Before, it was ‘I go out and find the tools’ myself. Now it’s ‘I say one sentence, and it gets it done’.
“Further down the line, it may be ‘I don’t need to say anything’.”
However, the tool’s heavy token usage made it expensive to run, according to Poe Zhao, a Beijing-based tech analyst and founder of Hello China Tech. In response, some Chinese cloud providers like Volcano Engine have introduced fixed-subscription options.
The global AI industry is moving towards developing AI agents capable of autonomously executing tasks on behalf of users. However, their strong autonomy as well as privacy concerns have raised questions about their regulatory future, including in China.
In December, ByteDance’s new AI-powered agentic phone faced pushback from users and domestic rivals, with several of China’s most widely used apps restricting its access.
In its announcement on Wednesday, ByteDance warned users about Moltbot’s security and privacy risks and urged them to regularly check their permission settings. “As the tool can access all device data, digital accounts and connect to the internet via multiple ports … it is recommended to deploy Moltbot on a dedicated device rather than one containing sensitive information,” the TikTok owner said.

data.title: Alibaba’s T-Head unit unveils details of AI chip designed to rival Nvidia’s GPUs
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding’s semiconductor design arm, T-Head, on Thursday unveiled details of its advanced artificial intelligence chip with capabilities said to be on par with Nvidia’s H20, days after the unit was reportedly being prepared for a potential spin-off and public listing.
According to information on T-Head’s website, the Zhenwu 810E was a “fully self-developed” parallel processing unit (PPU), an application-specific integrated circuit designed for both AI training and inference. Alibaba owns the SCMP.
Developed by T-Head to handle the massive data throughput required for generative AI systems, the Zhenwu 810E supports 96 gigabytes of High Bandwidth Memory 2 Enhanced – a 3D-stacked synchronous dynamic random-access memory standard designed for AI, machine learning and high-performance computing.
A single card with the T-Head PPU handles a bandwidth of 700 gigabytes per second, which made it on par with Nvidia’s H20 – a graphics processing unit (GPU) tailored by Nvidia specifically for China to comply with US export restrictions.
China last year summoned Nvidia over alleged “back door safety risks” in the H20, which signalled a de facto ban on its sales to local firms.
T-Head’s launch of the Zhenwu 810E marked the latest initiative by China’s Big Tech firms, including Huawei Technologies and Baidu, to develop a domestic alternative to Nvidia’s GPUs and bolster the country’s technology self-reliance efforts.
Before Washington and Beijing stepped up their regulatory scrutiny of the firm last year, Nvidia said it had 95 per cent of the advanced chip market in China.
Alibaba’s Hong Kong-listed stock was unchanged at HK$173.30 on Thursday.

While details of the Zhenwu 810E were made public this week for the first time, the advanced processor had already been deployed in multiple 10,000-chip computing clusters of Alibaba Cloud – the AI and cloud services unit of Hangzhou-based Alibaba – as well as large external clients, according to T-Head.
The T-Head-designed chip was first unveiled in a report by state broadcaster China Central Television that aired on September 16, which simply identified it as PPU. It showed Premier Li Qiang being briefed by China Unicom on the use of mainland-developed chips in the telecommunications network operator’s infrastructure.
The report showed T-Head’s PPU being compared with Nvidia’s H20 and A800 GPUs in a performance benchmark at a data centre operated by China Unicom in northwestern Qinghai province.
Founded in 2018, T-Head said its self-developed Inter-Chip Network technology provided high bandwidth and low latency training and inference for large language models. The Zhenwu 810E was also suitable for autonomous driving applications, it added.
The Zhenwu 810E, however, still lagged behind the performance of the H200, which delivers roughly six times the performance of the H20. Nvidia’s second most powerful GPU was expected to start shipping to Chinese clients from this week.
An initial H200 shipment of more than 400,000 units was expected to be delivered to ByteDance, Alibaba and Tencent Holdings, according to a Reuters report.

data.title: Town planning body slammed for neglecting disabled people over Wan Chai plan
data.description:
Disabled residents and a former lawmaker have slammed Hong Kong’s town planning authorities for failing to consider accessibility needs and overlooking potential fire safety risks in an approved development plan in Wan Chai, calling for a review to make the area more barrier-free.
Former social welfare sector lawmaker Tik Chi-yuen said on Thursday that the Town Planning Board-approved, high-density development plan covering Sau Wa Fong and Nam Koo Terrace failed to take into account its impact on those in need.
St Francis Street is a major thoroughfare in the neighbourhood connecting Queen’s Road East to the Starstreet Precinct, but its steep and narrow one-way path with a gradient of about 1:6 already poses barriers to residents with disabilities, including those using wheelchairs.
Tik noted the development under the Wan Chai Outline Zoning Plan would have more than 500 flats, and warned that the influx of residents could worsen traffic congestion on the street and heighten safety risks in the event of a fire.
He added that a planned barrier-free lift connecting Sau Wa Fong and St Francis Street would only take residents to the sloping street, meaning that the area would remain inaccessible to those with disabilities.
He said the designs violated the Disability Discrimination Ordinance and the principles of universal design intended to create a barrier-free environment.
“This project has created an undesirable precedent. The government only looked at one aspect of the development,” said Tik, who is also chairman of the Third Side party.
“True accessibility requires comprehensive and convenient access. Otherwise, the Disability Discrimination Ordinance and universal design are just empty words or even ‘toothless tigers’.”

Tik called on authorities to re-examine the development plan, taking into account the needs of those with disabilities and protecting residents’ safety.
He also urged the Equal Opportunities Commission, the city’s equality watchdog, to establish clear guidelines for redevelopment projects to ensure that disabled people’s needs were considered, and set up universal design plans for each district.
Wheelchair user Wong Tse-yan said the steep slope on St Francis Street was inaccessible to her, adding that she was also concerned about the potential dangers if a fire broke out in the neighbourhood.
Peter Wong Kam-ho, deputy chief executive officer of the Dialogue In The Dark (HK) Foundation, a charity dedicated to inclusion and equality, said walking on the sloping street during heavy traffic was a challenge for him and other people with visual impairments.
“Walking on slopes is not a problem for me, nor is climbing stairs, but my biggest worry is walking on the street because, as a blind person, I can’t see cars,” he said. “I can hear the cars coming and going fast on this street.”
The SCMP has reached out to the Planning Department for comment.

data.title: Pawsitively posh: Hong Kong Pet Show takes luxury to new heights
data.description:
Private jet holidays, AI-powered cat litter boxes and human-grade fresh dog food inspired by Hong Kong’s char siu rice are all the rage at this year’s Hong Kong Pet Show, as exhibitors express confidence in the purchasing power of the city’s pet owners.
Humans, dogs and cats jostled alongside each other on Thursday, the opening day of the expo at the Hong Kong Exhibition and Convention Centre, as furry friends decked out in outfits sampled treats like dried alligator and bully sticks.
More than 1,000 exhibitors are taking part in the show, which runs until Sunday.
Pet travel is one service that has seen exponential growth, with one agency nearly doubling sales from last year.
Life Travel managing director Billy Chan said taking a holiday with pets on a private jet was no longer the preserve of the elite and was gaining popularity among Hong Kong’s middle class.
“In 2025, we had 12 tours. This year, we already have 22 confirmed departures,” said Chan, adding that more competitors had entered the market since their launch in 2017, given the growth in demand.
“After the [Covid] pandemic, many owners who have the financial ability are very willing to travel with their pets.”
For example, a week-long Christmas trip to Tokyo with a pet – which includes flights on a private jet, paperwork and travel through the Hong Kong Business Aviation Centre – costs HK$95,000 (US$12,173).
Return flights to Osaka, Hokkaido and Seoul would cost at least HK$60,000 on the jet, which can accommodate around 14 pairs of owners and their pets, while the company is expected to launch its first Singapore tour later this year.

Life Travel is prioritising rabies-free destinations, so Hong Kong pets can avoid quarantine after returning to the city.
Singapore-based Pet Cube, which sells human-grade fresh dog food and pet-friendly ice cream, is back at the show for a second year with two limited-edition flavours inspired by Hong Kong’s roast dishes.
Braised duck rice, made with a special pet-safe five spice blend, and char siu rice are highlights of Pet Cube’s cha chaan teng-themed booth at the show.
The frozen meals, which cost around HK$60 per serving, are prepared without sugar, salt and preservatives, making them a more nutritious option for pets, said Francis Cabana, the company’s director of nutrition.
Cabana said the company hopes to raise awareness about the health benefits of fresh food among pet owners and has confidence in Hong Kong’s consumers and their willingness to spend on their pets.
“A lot of people are curious about feeding fresh food, but they do not know where to start,” he said. “Hong Kong owners are smart, so they want the best for their pet. They know what is good and what is bad and what will benefit long-term health.”
Mainland brands producing hi-tech pet products are also targeting the Hong Kong market, including Shanghai-based Petkit, which specialises in products for cat owners such as AI-powered litter boxes and water fountains.
Ken Yau, a senior sales assistant at distributor Pet Planet Trading, hopes to see a 5 per cent increase in sales this year, as this technology has proven popular with Hongkongers who frequently travel out of the city.
The automated litter box for multi-cat homes, which uses AI to analyse waste, stool consistency, identify individual cats, retails for HK$3,999.
It automatically cleans, auto-seals every trash bag and refills a new one while owners can monitor their pets through a camera linked to a mobile app.

Carl Wong, the chairman of show organiser Exhibition Group, said the global pet economy was on track to hit US$134.46 billion (HK$1.04 trillion) this year amid sustained growth.
“With the government actively pushing for pet-friendly policies and streamlining quarantine requirements, the pet trade is presented with brand new opportunities,” he said.
Within two hours of the show’s opening, shopper Wing Lee had spent about HK$6,000 on her two 11-year-old Shiba Inu dogs including HK$2,899 on a stroller, one of the big-ticket items on her to-buy list at the expo.
The 23-year-old hospitality worker had a shopping trolley filled to the brim with snacks, supplements and cleaning products.
“I don’t set a budget. If I think the products are good, I will buy them,” she said, adding she looks forward to Hong Kong turning into a pet-friendlier city with the government’s new measures.
“I believe that having pets is now the new norm instead of having kids.”

data.title: Starmer stresses Hong Kong’s stability as key to stronger ties in meeting with Xi
data.description:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer has told Chinese President Xi Jinping that Hong Kong’s prosperity and stability are in the shared interests of both China and the United Kingdom in a high-profile meeting during his four-day trip to Beijing.
Starmer, the first British leader to visit China since 2018, confirmed after the meeting, when asked by the media, that he had mentioned the landmark national security case of jailed former media boss Jimmy Lai Chee-ying with Xi.
According to the state news agency Xinhua, Starmer commended Hong Kong’s role in Sino-British relations during the 80-minute meeting.
“Hong Kong’s prosperity and stability are in the common interests of both countries,” Starmer said. “The UK welcomes Hong Kong as a unique and important bridge between the UK and China.”
However, the report did not state that the British leader had brought up Lai’s case or human rights issues.
Speaking to the media after the meeting, Starmer confirmed that he had raised Lai’s national security trial with Xi, in which the founder of the now-defunct Apple Daily tabloid was convicted and could face life imprisonment.
Starmer made the remarks only when pressed by reporters during his visit to the Forbidden City on Thursday as to whether he had raised Lai’s trial and the treatment of Uygurs, despite Lai’s British citizenship.

“Part of the rationale for engagement is to make sure that we can both seize the opportunities that are available, which is what we’ve done, but also have a mature discussion about issues that we disagree on,” Starmer said. “We did have a respectful discussion about that.”
Lai’s trial has drawn heavy international scrutiny, with US President Donald Trump urging Xi to release him, while the British government has also summoned China’s ambassador to the UK to protest against the conviction.
Lau Siu-kai, a consultant for the semi-official Chinese Association of Hong Kong and Macau Studies think tank, said Hong Kong had long been a source of tension between the UK and Beijing, but Britain must re-engage with China amid what he described as economic coercion from the US.
“The way the UK handled the case of Jimmy Lai should be the touchstone of the UK’s sincerity in bringing about a better relationship with China,” Lau said.
He added it was “entirely expected” that Starmer would raise Lai’s case and broader human rights concerns with Xi.
“He could not avoid mentioning it, he had to account to his home country,” Lau said. “Bringing it up is not an issue, whether any real action follows is what matters. The crux is whether Britain will take action against Hong Kong and China after Lai is sentenced.”
He said that if Britain refrained from exploiting Hong Kong as a wedge against Beijing and instead adopted a friendlier stance, the city could play a pivotal role in strengthening China–UK relations.

Alfred Wu Muluan, an associate professor at the National University of Singapore’s Lee Kuan Yew School of Public Policy, said Starmer’s relatively mild approach to Hong Kong was expected, given the visit’s primary focus on economic ties and repairing UK–China relations.
“The UK and Europe are much more keen to strike a balance, I think, between China and the US. Given the current strong position of Donald Trump, I think it’s just for balance,” Wu said.
“I think both sides don’t want to talk [about Hong Kong]. Because after all, at this stage, much more focus is on the economy, why do they need to talk about Hong Kong?”
He said that although Lai’s case had been raised, it was simply a reiteration of the UK’s position, adding that Starmer only confirmed that he had brought up the matter when questioned by reporters after the meeting, and Xinhua’s omission of the topic suggested that both sides preferred to keep the discussion low profile.
“They are not actually really taking any substantial actions on this issue,” Wu said.
Wu noted that improved Sino-British relations following Starmer’s visit could also lead to a gradual thaw in ties between Hong Kong and the UK, particularly on the economic front.
